title: FINAL APPROVED TRANSLATIONS — BEWARE OF THE CAR (1966)

items.speaker: Amateur theater director

original.rus: Не пора ли, друзья мои, нам замахнуться на Вильяма, понимаете, нашего Шекспира?

translations.eng: Isn’t it time, my friends, we took a swing at William — Shakespeare, that is.

items.analysis: The humor comes from inflated rhetorical style applied to an amateur theatrical ambition. The solemn, collective tone ('друзья мои') clashes with the familiar phrasing 'our Shakespeare,' parodying Soviet grandiloquence and at the same time recognizing the immense role that translations of Shakespeare played for Russian culture. It is very funny because Shakespeare is of course a foreign author, and it is very heartwarming to call him `our`.

items.speaker: Investigator / Random person from the crowd

original.rus: — Кто свидетель? — Я! А что случилось?

translations.eng: Who’s the witness? — Me! …What’s going on?

items.analysis: The joke hinges on automatic civic compliance followed by confusion. The character volunteers as a witness before knowing the situation, exposing a reflexive bureaucratic environment where roles are assumed first and understood later.

items.speaker: Dima / Investigator

original.rus: — У кого нога? — У того, у кого надо нога.

translations.eng: Who’s the one with the leg? — The right person.

items.analysis: The exchange plays on evasive logic and tautology. Note that there is no possession here; the detective simply wants to say that he trusts the person who was injured and so in this case, it cannot possibly have anything to do with the case. The translation must not focus on the leg but on the person.

items.speaker: Semyon Vassilyevich

original.rus: Тебя посодют, а ты не воруй!

translations.eng: You steal — you get locked up.

items.analysis: The line condenses folk morality and state threat into a blunt proverb-like sentence. It is important here that this is not any kind of warning for the future despite the imperative; the actual meaning of this idiomatic Russian construction is more like `you should not have been stealing; now face your punishment!' Its comic force lies in the non-standard grammar ('посодют') combined with absolute legal certainty, creating a mixture of naïveté and institutional menace.

items.speaker: Dima / Semyon Vassilyevich

original.rus: — Семён Васильевич, вы же у меня в доме! — Твой дом — тюрма!

translations.eng: Semyon Vasilyevich, this is my house. — Your house is jail.

items.analysis: The abrupt declarative structure intensifies Semyon Vassilyevich's insult directed at his long-suffering son-in-law who is still trying to be polite with him. It is important here that both lines use the same word to refer to the concept of the house.

items.speaker: Dima / Semyon Vassilyevich

original.rus: — Что делать? Что делать? — Сухари сушить!

translations.eng: What do we do? What do we do? — Pack a toothbrush.

items.analysis: The question echoes Chernyshevsky’s canonical 'What is to be done?', but the answer trivializes it into prison preparation. The humor depends on ideological deflation: high intellectual tradition collapses into practical anticipation of arrest. Note: to a non-Russian viewer, a literal translation will probably be opaque.

items.speaker: Dima
items.analysis: Every viewer would sympathize with Dima at this point who is trying to be polite with his father-in-law and quite genuinely suffers from Semyon Vassilyevich's character.

items.speaker: Semyon Vassilyevich
items.analysis: The slogan mimics political protest rhetoric. Its comic and dramatic effect lies in elevating Detochkin into a symbolic dissident figure, blending parody of mass demonstrations with genuine moral sympathy.
